**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, while incentives better predict performance quantity, and the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. Supports interest mattering a lot, but not "almost exclusively."

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012), "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research" (Perspectives on Psychological Science)**
Interest–job fit meaningfully predicts performance, persistence, and career success, with larger effects than previously assumed. One of the strongest empirical bases for your friends' claim.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction across studies. Suggests "a bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (PNAS)**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus beyond a comfortable income threshold, though life evaluation keeps rising. At OpenAI/Google compensation levels, marginal salary differences likely affect well-being very little.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Sustained motivation depends on three needs — autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interestingness alone. Notably, leading a team (something you've always wanted) could satisfy these needs even if the work content is less fascinating.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed cultivate passion in new domains. Complicates your friends' framing: the Google role's interestingness isn't necessarily static.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review)**
Employees substantially reshape the meaning and content of their roles through "job crafting." A team-lead position may give you more latitude to make work interesting than an individual contributor role.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who experience work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Supports weighting meaning/interest over salary — but note the "career" orientation (advancement, leadership) also predicted satisfaction better than pay orientation.

**Bottom line:** The research supports weighting interest heavily and salary lightly — but not "almost exclusively"; autonomy, growth toward long-held goals (like leading a team), and your ability to develop interest in new work are also well-supported factors.

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
A meta-analysis of 86 studies found that pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction. This supports the idea that a moderately higher salary is unlikely to meaningfully improve how you feel about your work day-to-day.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (PNAS)**
Studying over 10,000 cadets, the authors found that internal motives (genuine interest in the work) predicted long-term success, while instrumental motives (status, money) not only failed to help but actually weakened outcomes when mixed with internal ones. Evidence that prioritizing interest over instrumental rewards has real long-run payoff.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being" (American Psychologist)**
The foundational paper arguing that sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team could increase autonomy and relatedness, so "interestingness" is one input among several, not the only one.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The power of small wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 daily diary entries showed that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress in meaningful work. Suggests that whether you'll make tangible progress in either role matters as much as how interesting the topic sounds.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed give up when work gets difficult, while those who believe interests can be developed sustain engagement across domains. Implies the Google role might become more interesting than it appears now, especially if leading a team is a long-held aspiration.

**Johnston & Lee (2013), "Extra status and extra stress: Are promotions good for us?" (Industrial and Labor Relations Review)**
Using longitudinal data, the authors found that promotions to roles with more responsibility improved job satisfaction only temporarily while measurably worsening mental health and time pressure. A caution that "leading a small team" can feel different in practice than in anticipation.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances (like raises or new titles) will make them feel, overweighting salient features and underweighting daily experience. Relevant in both directions: you may be overestimating the appeal of the title and salary, or romanticizing the "interestingness" of your current work.

**Bottom line:** Your friends are directionally right — research consistently shows interest and intrinsic motivation are more predictive of satisfaction and performance than pay or status — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, growth, and relatedness (which team leadership might provide) carry comparable weight in the literature.

answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' view:**

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?"** — Distinguishes "motivators" (interesting work, achievement, growth) from "hygiene factors" (salary, status), finding that only the former drive lasting satisfaction. Salary increases prevent dissatisfaction but don't create engagement.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** — Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around $75K (roughly $110K today), well below either of your offers. **Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation"** — Well-being depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness. Leading a team can satisfy competence and relatedness needs that pure IC research work may not.

The "less interesting" Google work may become interesting, especially with the novelty of leadership.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** — Employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning, often turning mundane roles into engaging ones. How interesting a job is depends partly on what you do with it, not just the job description.

**Gilbert & Wilson (2000), "Miswanting: Some Problems in the Forecasting of Future Affective States"** — People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like "interestingness" right now) and underweighting adaptation. Your forecast of how much you'll miss interesting work — or enjoy leading — is probably less accurate than you think.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"** — Job satisfaction is best predicted by overall person-job and person-organization fit across multiple dimensions, not any single factor. No serious meta-analysis supports weighting one factor "almost exclusively."

**Bottom line:** The research supports your friends directionally — interesting work matters more than people assume and salary less — but not the "almost exclusively" part. The literature suggests the real question is whether leading a team (a long-held goal of yours, satisfying autonomy and growth needs) might itself become your new "interesting."

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist***

A meta-analysis showing interest–job fit meaningfully predicts performance and persistence, with stronger effects than often assumed. Supports the idea that interest is underrated as a predictor of doing well, not just feeling well.

**Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020) — "Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Found that interest fit correlates with job satisfaction only modestly (r ≈ .19), weaker than many expect. Suggests "follow your interests" is not a guarantee of satisfaction—other factors matter substantially.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Pay level correlates very weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15) and barely at all with pay satisfaction itself. Supports discounting the salary difference in your decision.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just intrinsic interest in the task. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness needs that pure IC research work may not.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
The Job Characteristics Model shows motivation comes from skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback combined—"interestingness" is only part of the picture. A leadership role can score higher on several of these dimensions even if the subject matter is duller.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who see work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Relevant caveat: calling orientation is partly about how you frame work, and people can craft callings in roles that didn't initially seem fascinating.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting" (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)**
People systematically mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting one salient dimension. Both "it's less interesting" and "I'll finally lead a team" are exactly the kind of salient features people over-index on.

**Schkade & Kahneman (1998) — "Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion" (Psychological Science)**
Whatever you're focusing on while deciding seems more important than it will be once you're living the choice. Right now you're focusing on "interestingness"—partly because your friends primed you to.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, especially for complex work like research. But it operates jointly with incentives rather than being the sole factor.

---

Bottom line: the research supports your friends *directionally* (interest > salary for satisfaction and performance) but not the "almost exclusively" part—autonomy, growth, and fulfilling a long-held goal like leading a team are well-supported drivers too, and meta-analytic effect sizes for interest fit alone are modest.

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
A meta-analysis of 92 studies found that pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This supports the idea that a moderate salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully change how you feel about your job day-to-day.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers of motivating work: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interestingness" maps to only some of these; a team-lead role might score higher on autonomy and significance.

People who view work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those with job or career orientations, largely independent of occupation or income. This supports your friends' point that meaning/interest matters more than external markers.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, while incentives predict quantity-type performance. However, the two are not mutually exclusive — extrinsic rewards don't necessarily crowd out intrinsic motivation as much as popularly believed.

Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Interest matters, but the structure that enables visible progress matters just as much.

People dramatically overweight whatever dimension they're currently focusing on when predicting future satisfaction. Relevant caution for you in both directions: you may be overweighting "interestingness" or overweighting "finally leading a team."

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting"**
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events, including job changes. Both the excitement of a new role and the loss of interesting work tend to fade faster than expected.

---

One honest caveat: the research supports that interesting/meaningful work is *important* and that salary is *overrated*, but I'm not aware of evidence that interest should be weighted "almost exclusively." The literature (especially SDT and Hackman & Oldham) suggests autonomy, growth, and relationships — things a leadership role might provide — carry comparable weight.

I'll add a meta-analysis on intrinsic versus extrinsic motivation showing both matter for performance. I'm thinking I should organize these into two sections: papers supporting the friends' position and papers that complicate it, with brief two-sentence summaries for each, then maybe a quick closing line since they asked specifically for papers and summaries.
answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim:**

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**
This meta-analysis found intrinsic motivation is a strong and consistent predictor of performance, especially for quality of work (versus quantity). However, it also found extrinsic incentives matter and aren't simply destructive, complicating the "exclusively focus on interest" framing.

**"Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research" — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)**
Interest-job fit meaningfully predicts both performance and persistence in a role, with stronger effects than earlier reviews suggested. The authors argue interests were historically underrated in selection and career research.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**
People who view their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher work and life satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations. Notably, this held even within the same occupation.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**
Pay level correlates with job satisfaction only weakly (r ≈ .15) across 86 studies. This suggests a modest salary bump is unlikely to drive lasting satisfaction differences.

**Complicating your friends' claim:**

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" — Ryan & Deci (2000)**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not interestingness alone. A leadership role you've always wanted could serve autonomy and competence needs that "interesting work as an individual contributor" may not.

**"Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" — O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018)**
Interest is substantially developed through engagement rather than simply found pre-existing. Work that seems less interesting upfront (e.g., a new leadership challenge) can become genuinely interesting as skill and investment grow.

**"What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?" — Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012)**
People systematically choose options that differ from what they predict would maximize their happiness, valuing things like purpose, status, and family alongside happiness. This suggests "maximize interestingness" is too narrow a decision rule for what people actually care about.

**"Affective Forecasting" — Wilson & Gilbert (2003)**
People reliably overestimate how intensely and how long any single factor of a decision will affect their happiness (the "impact bias"). Both "interesting work" and "leading a team" will likely matter less, and adapt faster, than you currently predict.

**Bottom line implied by the literature:** interest is genuinely important and arguably underrated relative to pay—but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and long-held goals (like leading a team) carry real weight, and interest itself can develop in new roles.

(2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS).** Cadets with internal motives had better long-term outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (money, status) actually *diminished* the benefits of internal ones. **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (Psychological Bulletin)

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** (Journal of Vocational Behavior)

**Papers supporting your friends' claim:**

People systematically underweight intrinsic rewards like interest when making decisions about future activities, but those rewards turn out to dominate their actual experience and persistence once inside the activity. This is the closest empirical support for "interestingness is underrated."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** (Journal of Vocational Behavior). Across 92 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. A "bit higher salary" predicts almost nothing about how satisfied you'll be.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (Psychological Bulletin). Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially performance *quality* (the kind that matters in research). However, extrinsic incentives still independently predict outcomes — undermining the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** (PNAS). Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how people rate their lives abstractly but not day-to-day emotional experience. At OpenAI/Google compensation levels, you are far past that threshold.

**Papers complicating your friends' claim:**

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"** (Psychological Inquiry). Well-being depends on three needs — autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Leading a team, something you say you've always wanted, can satisfy autonomy and competence needs that pure research work may not.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (Psychological Science). People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard; people who believe interests are developed sustain engagement in new domains. "Less interesting" work often becomes interesting once you build competence in it — including management.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** (Academy of Management Review). People actively reshape the tasks and relationships of their jobs to make them more meaningful and engaging. The interestingness of a role is partly endogenous, not a fixed property you're choosing between.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting"** (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology). People reliably mispredict the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events due to focalism — fixating on one salient feature of a choice. Your current fixation on "interesting vs. not" may itself be a forecasting error in either direction.

**Chan & Drasgow (2001), "Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead"** (Journal of Applied Psychology). **Bottom line the literature suggests:** your friends are half right — interest is genuinely underweighted relative to salary — but "almost exclusively" isn't supported, especially when the alternative fulfills a long-held goal like leading a team.

**Papers supporting your friends' view:**

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Wrzesniewski et al., 1997)** — People who view their work as intrinsically meaningful (a "calling") report significantly higher work and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. (O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, 2018)** — People who believe passions are "found" (fixed) give up faster when work gets hard; interests can be developed in new domains over time. **"Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features" (Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007)** — Meta-analysis showing satisfaction is predicted by a bundle of factors: autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and social support — not task interestingness alone. ---

**Bottom line:** Your friends are directionally right — intrinsic interest is one of the strongest and most underweighted predictors of satisfaction and performance, while salary deltas are weak ones. **Wilson & Gilbert (2003). **Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist***
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts well-being and engagement, but it depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness—not interest alone. Leading a team you've always wanted could satisfy autonomy and competence needs that "interestingness" doesn't capture.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin***

People who believe interests are fixed ("find your passion") give up faster when work gets hard; those who believe interests are developed persist and find new domains engaging. **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality***
People who view their work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations. Favors your friends' position—if OpenAI work feels like a calling, that's a meaningful signal.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being," *PNAS***
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus well before high-tech salaries, so a marginal raise at your income level likely buys little happiness. Weakens the salary argument for Google specifically.

**Vallerand et al. (2003), "Les Passions de l'Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion," *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology***
Harmonious passion (work integrated flexibly into life) predicts well-being, while obsessive passion predicts burnout and conflict. A caution against optimizing for "interesting" so hard that it crowds out everything else.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis," *Personnel Psychology***
Person–job and person–organization fit are among the strongest predictors of satisfaction, commitment, and retention. "Fit" includes values, growth aspirations (e.g., wanting to lead), and culture—not just task interest.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting," *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology***
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of how future choices will make them feel, overweighting one salient feature of a decision. Both "interesting work" and "leading a team" are likely subject to this focusing illusion.

**Bottom line:** the research supports your friends *directionally* (intrinsic interest is genuinely powerful and money is overrated), but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, growth, fit, and your long-held leadership goal are independently well-supported predictors of satisfaction.

answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim (interest/intrinsic motivation matters more than people think):**

**"Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" — Wrzesniewski et al., PNAS, 2014**
Cadets with strong internal motives (genuine interest in the work) were more likely to graduate, become commissioned officers, and stay in the military. Notably, holding instrumental motives (career advancement, pay) *alongside* internal ones predicted *worse* outcomes, not better.

**"The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis" — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, Journal of Vocational Behavior, 2010**
Across 86 studies, the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction was only about r = .15 — surprisingly weak. People at very different pay levels report similar satisfaction, suggesting marginal salary differences are overweighted in career decisions.

**"A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation" — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, Psychological Bulletin, 1999**
Across 128 experiments, contingent extrinsic rewards tended to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. This supports the idea that chasing external incentives can erode the very engagement that drives long-term performance.

**"Vocational interests and performance: A quantitative summary" — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, Perspectives on Psychological Science, 2012**
Interest–job fit meaningfully predicts both performance and persistence, with stronger effects than many traditional predictors suggest. Interest isn't just about enjoyment — it's a legitimate predictor of how well you'll actually do.

**Complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**"Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being" — Ryan & Deci, American Psychologist, 2000**
Sustained motivation rests on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness* — not interestingness alone. A leadership role you've always wanted could satisfy autonomy and growth needs that "interesting work" by itself doesn't.

**"Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" — O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, Psychological Science, 2018**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those with a "growth" theory of interest develop passion in new domains. This cuts against assuming the Google role's interestingness is static — interest can be cultivated, especially in a role aligned with your goals.

**"Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary" — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, Journal of Applied Psychology, 2007**
Job satisfaction is driven by a bundle of characteristics — autonomy, task significance, feedback, social support — not task interest alone. Task variety and significance (e.g., leading people) independently predict satisfaction and commitment.

**"Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" — Killingsworth, PNAS, 2021**
Contrary to the famous Kahneman & Deaton plateau finding, experienced well-being continued rising log-linearly with income with no plateau. Money matters less than people assume, but it isn't irrelevant.

**"Affective forecasting" — Wilson & Gilbert, Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 2003**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overestimating both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions. This applies to *both* options — you may be overestimating how much you'd miss the interesting work *and* how much you'd enjoy leading a team.

**Bottom line:** the literature directionally supports your friends — interest is genuinely underrated relative to pay — but "almost exclusively" is an overstatement, since autonomy, growth, and the fact that interest can be developed all matter too.

prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Google - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I need to find actual research papers on intrinsic motivation and meaningful work to answer the user's question fairly. While the claim that interesting things are underrated has some merit in the research, I should acknowledge that the evidence is more complex than an exclusive focus would suggest. Let me pull together real papers with their summaries in the format they requested. I'm also pulling in O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton on how interest can be developed rather than discovered, and Nikolova & Cnossen showing that autonomy, relatedness, and competence matter more than pay for meaningfulness. The self-concordance model supports the leadership aspiration angle too — goals aligned with personal values drive well-being. So the evidence suggests interest is important but not dominant; autonomy, growth, and self-aligned goals matter significantly, and interest itself can be cultivated. I'll format this as a concise list of 7-8 papers with two-sentence summaries each, then give a direct one-line answer to whether the claim is true.
answer:
Short answer: the research supports "intrinsic interest matters a lot," but not "focus on it almost exclusively." Here's the relevant literature:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being"** — Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not interest alone. A leadership role you've long wanted may satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if the subject matter is less fascinating.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis"** — Finds intrinsic motivation strongly predicts quality of performance, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity. Supports your friends partially: for creative research work specifically, intrinsic interest is a major performance driver.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis"** — Pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction. This is the strongest evidence in your friends' favor: a "bit higher salary" should weigh very little in your decision.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?"** — Shows that interests are developed, not just discovered, and people who believe passion is "found" abandon things when they get hard. Undermines a strict version of your friends' claim: the Google role might become interesting as you grow into it.

**Sheldon & Elliot (1999), "Goal striving, need satisfaction, and longitudinal well-being: The self-concordance model"** — Pursuing goals aligned with your authentic values produces lasting well-being gains; pursuing externally imposed goals doesn't. Relevant because leading a team is *your* long-held goal, which makes it self-concordant — that counts in its favor, not against.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work"** — Employees actively reshape task and relational boundaries to make work more meaningful. Suggests "interestingness" isn't fixed at offer time; you could potentially craft either role toward what engages you.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The power of small wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)** — Day-to-day motivation is driven most by making visible progress in meaningful work. Implies you should ask which role offers clearer progress and meaning, not just which topic sounds more exciting.

**Nikolova & Cnossen (2020), "What makes work meaningful and why economists should care about it"** — Autonomy, competence, and relatedness explain far more variance in work meaningfulness than pay or benefits. Reinforces that the question is which job better satisfies those three needs overall.

**Verdict:** Your friends are overstating it. The evidence says (1) salary should weigh little, (2) intrinsic interest matters a great deal for research work, but (3) a self-concordant goal like leading a team is also a well-validated driver of satisfaction, and interest can grow in a role you initially find duller. The real comparison is autonomy + competence + meaning + alignment with your own goals across both offers — not interestingness alone.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Google - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I need to gather some well-established research papers on job satisfaction and intrinsic motivation to answer this question properly. I'm thinking about Amabile's work on intrinsic motivation, Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory, Wrzesniewski's research on calling in work, and some key meta-analyses on pay and satisfaction. I should stick to real, recognizable studies rather than inventing citations. I'm adding more papers to round out the evidence: Cerasoli's meta-analysis shows both intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives matter together, which directly challenges the "almost exclusively" framing; Humphrey's work on task design features like autonomy and significance also predict satisfaction; and Levitt's coin-flip study on career switching reveals people who make major changes report higher happiness. I should include around seven papers total that collectively demonstrate interest matters greatly but isn't the only factor, and that growth opportunities like leadership roles also play a role.
answer:
**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"** — Foundational job characteristics model showing that task variety, significance, autonomy, and feedback drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Notably, autonomy (which often comes with leading a team) is one of the strongest predictors, not just task interestingness.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation"** — Meta-analysis of 128 experiments finding that contingent monetary rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Supports your friends' direction, though it concerns motivation dynamics, not career-choice outcomes.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature"** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction. Suggests a modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully move your day-to-day satisfaction.

**Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006), "Would you be happier if you were richer? A focusing illusion"** — People systematically overestimate how much income changes will affect their happiness, because they focus on the salient comparison at decision time. Relevant warning for weighting the salary difference heavily.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis"** — Both intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives matter, with intrinsic motivation better predicting quality of work and incentives better predicting quantity. Directly contradicts the "almost exclusively" framing — the evidence is for "both, with intrinsic mattering more for quality."

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work"** — People who see their work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. However, orientation is partly about how you relate to work, not just the work's objective interestingness.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want"** — People are poor at predicting the intensity and duration of future emotions, both about gains (salary, title) and about presumed downsides (less interesting work). Cuts both ways: you may also be overestimating how bored you'd be, or how much you'd enjoy managing.

**Ng, Eby, Sorensen & Feldman (2005), "Predictors of objective and subjective career success: A meta-analysis"** — Objective success (pay, promotions) and subjective success (satisfaction) have largely different predictors and are only weakly related. A reminder that "leading a team" satisfies a different need (a long-held aspiration) than task interest does — these aren't on the same scale.

Bottom line: the research supports weighting interestingness heavily over salary, but not "almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth, and fulfilling long-held aspirations (like leading a team) are independently strong predictors of satisfaction.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Google - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I need to be careful here — I shouldn't make up papers just to support their claim. Instead, I should reference actual, well-established research on what drives job satisfaction and motivation. Self-Determination Theory by Deci and Ryan is a solid starting point, along with other recognized work on intrinsic versus extrinsic motivation and what actually makes people satisfied in their careers. I'm also considering affective forecasting research on how we mispredict what makes us happy, and regret studies about inaction versus action. The friends' claim about "almost exclusively" intrinsic motivation needs nuance—the research shows intrinsic interest matters greatly, but relatedness, autonomy, growth, and his leadership aspirations also play real roles. I should stick to the requested format of paper names with two-sentence summaries, maybe with a brief closing takeaway. Let me finalize around 7-9 papers that balance these perspectives while staying accurate on the citations and effect sizes.
answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist).** Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — intrinsic interest is one major input, not the only one. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and competence needs, which complicates your friends' claim.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior).** Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction. Supports your friends' point that a modest salary bump is a weak predictor of how happy you'll be.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (PNAS).** Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k, though life evaluation keeps rising with income. At your likely compensation level, marginal salary differences probably matter little for daily happiness.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved" (PNAS).** An adversarial collaboration showing happiness actually does keep rising with income for most people, contradicting the strict plateau finding. A caution against treating "money doesn't matter" as settled science.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS).** Cadets driven by internal motives outperformed those who also held strong instrumental motives (money, career advancement). This is probably the strongest evidence for your friends' position — though it's one population and has faced replication scrutiny.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin).** Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives independently predict quantity-type performance — they coexist rather than one being "underrated." Undermines the "almost exclusively" framing.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance).** Job satisfaction stems from skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not just intrinsic interest in the subject matter. A team-lead role could score higher on several of these dimensions despite "less interesting" content.

**Gilovich & Medvec (1995), "The Experience of Regret: What, When, and Why" (Psychological Review).** People's long-term regrets are dominated by inactions — things they always wanted to do but didn't. Relevant given you said leading a team is "something I always wanted."

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science).** People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like "interestingness") and underweighting adaptation. Both you and your friends are likely forecasting your future happiness imperfectly.

---

Honest verdict: the research supports "interesting work matters more than people assume" but **not** "almost exclusively." The strongest tension in your case isn't money vs. interest — it's interest vs. a long-held aspiration to lead, and the regret literature cuts against your friends there.

reasoning:

There's also the job satisfaction-performance meta-analysis by Judge and colleagues, Kahneman and Deaton's findings on income and well-being, and Cerasoli's meta-analysis showing intrinsic motivation predicts quality of work. I'm also considering O'Keefe's work on how interests develop rather than just being innate, and Nye's vocational fit research. I'm noting that Gilbert and Wilson's affective forecasting research is relevant but the thought cuts off before I finish that point.

The key insight emerging is that your friends' claim oversimplifies—research shows intrinsic interest matters significantly but extrinsic incentives also predict outcomes, and interests themselves are malleable. The Hackman and Oldham job characteristics model adds that work design itself influences motivation through meaningfulness and autonomy.

For the response format, I'm planning to list papers with bold titles, authors, and years, followed by two-sentence summaries that capture the nuance—some support the friends' position while others complicate it. I'll keep it strict to their request but consider adding a brief closing line to tie together the net takeaway.
answer:
**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives independently matter too—particularly for quantity-based outcomes. So the evidence supports intrinsic interest being very important, but not "almost exclusively" so.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014) — "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Cadets with strong internal motives (interest in the work itself) had better long-term outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (money, status) actually predicted *worse* outcomes. This is probably the strongest support for your friends' position.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012) — "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research" (Perspectives on Psychological Science)**
Meta-analysis showing that interest–job fit meaningfully predicts performance and persistence, with effects larger than previously assumed. Interest isn't just about enjoyment; it predicts how well you actually do.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) — "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (PNAS)**
Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how you *rate* your life but not your day-to-day emotional experience. A "bit higher salary" at your likely compensation level is unlikely to change your daily well-being.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests can be developed sustain engagement in new areas. Relevant caveat for you: the Google role might *become* interesting, especially given your long-standing desire to lead.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
Job satisfaction and internal motivation are driven by multiple characteristics: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—not interestingness alone. Leading a team can boost autonomy and significance, dimensions the "interesting" job may lack.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Contingent extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. A cautionary note for taking a less interesting job primarily for pay.

**Net read:** Your friends are directionally right—intrinsic interest is one of the best-supported predictors of long-term performance and satisfaction—but "almost exclusively" overstates the literature, which also shows interests are developable and that autonomy/growth (like leading a team) are independent drivers of motivation.

answer:
**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** (Ryan & Deci, 2000, *American Psychologist*)
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts well-being and persistence, but the theory identifies three needs—autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*—not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role could satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if the work content is less fascinating.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** (Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012, *Perspectives on Psychological Science*)
Interest–job fit predicts performance and persistence with moderate correlations (~0.3). This supports interest mattering meaningfully, but the effect sizes don't justify "almost exclusive" weighting over other factors.

Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, while incentives better predict quantity—and crucially, the two operate jointly rather than in opposition. The "jointly" finding cuts against your friends' exclusivity claim.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** (O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, 2018, *Psychological Science*)
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement in initially less exciting areas. This suggests the Google role might become interesting—management interest, in particular, often develops through doing it.

People who see work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those with job or career orientations. However, the calling orientation was found across occupations, suggesting it depends partly on how you frame your work, not only the work itself.

Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how people evaluate their lives but not day-to-day emotional experience. At OpenAI/Google compensation levels, a "bit higher salary" is likely the least decision-relevant variable here.

People systematically overestimate the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events—both the thrill of interesting work and the satisfaction of a promotion fade faster than predicted. This applies symmetrically to both of your options.

---

One sentence of editorializing: the literature supports interest as a *strong* factor but not an "almost exclusive" one—your friends are overstating it, and your long-held desire to lead a team maps onto the autonomy/growth needs the same research says matter.

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"** (Journal of Vocational Behavior)

Intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance quality, but incentives still mattered—the two coexist rather than one dominating. Evidence against the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

People systematically overestimate how strongly and how long any single change (a raise, a title) will affect their happiness. Applies symmetrically: you may also be overestimating how bad "less interesting" will feel once you're absorbed in leading a team.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*)**
Meta-analysis finding that pay level correlates only ~.15 with job satisfaction. Supports your friends' position insofar as the "bit higher salary" likely matters far less than you'd intuit.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012), "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research" (*Perspectives on Psychological Science*)**
Meta-analysis showing interest–job fit meaningfully predicts performance and persistence, with effects larger than previously assumed. Partial support for the friends: interest isn't just about enjoyment, it predicts how good you'll be.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (*Journal of Research in Personality*)**
People who see work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations. However, the orientation depends on how *you* relate to the work — leading a team could be a calling if leadership is what you've always wanted.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (*Psychological Science*)**
Shows that interests are developed rather than simply "found," and people with a fixed theory of interest give up too early on new domains. A direct counterpoint to your friends: the Google role might *become* interesting, especially given your latent interest in leadership.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis" (*Personnel Psychology*)**
Person–job fit — including needs–supplies fit (does the job give you what you want?) — is among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and low turnover. The key question this raises: which job better supplies *all* your needs, including the leadership aspiration?

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (*Current Directions in Psychological Science*)**
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events, and overweight whichever factor is salient at decision time. A caution against your friends' "almost exclusively" framing — and against any single-factor decision rule, in either direction.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (*Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)**
Job satisfaction is driven by multiple job characteristics — skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not task interestingness alone. Team leadership can score high on several of these dimensions even when the underlying work is less novel.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right (interest beats marginal salary) but wrong on "almost exclusively" — interest is developable, multidimensional fit matters, and an unfulfilled long-term aspiration like leadership is exactly the kind of factor people underweight when the current job's interestingness is salient.

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 92 independent samples, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This supports the idea that a modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully change how you feel about your work.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not on interestingness alone. Notably, a leadership role could increase autonomy and relatedness, so this paper cuts *against* "focus exclusively on interesting work."

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, especially for complex tasks like research. However, it operates *jointly* with extrinsic incentives rather than rendering them irrelevant.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. But orientation depends partly on how you frame and craft your role, not just on the task content itself.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Meaningfulness and progress — not novelty or "interestingness" per se — were key.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting"**
People systematically overestimate the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events, including career changes. Both your excitement about leading a team and your dread of "boring" work are likely exaggerated predictions.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005) — "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"**
Person–job and person–organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention. "Fit" is multidimensional — values, needs, and abilities — not reducible to how interesting the tasks are.

**Bottom line implied by the literature:** your friends are directionally right (intrinsic interest beats marginal pay), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, relationships, and progress matter comparably, and a team-lead role you've always wanted may score high on exactly those dimensions.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being"** (American Psychologist)
The foundational paper arguing that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive intrinsic motivation and well-being. Notably, it doesn't say "interestingness" alone matters — relatedness and competence growth (which a leadership role might provide) are equally central.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis"** (Psychological Bulletin)
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives matter too, especially for quantity-type outcomes — the two operate jointly rather than one being "underrated." This cuts against the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work"** (Journal of Research in Personality)
People who view work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those viewing it as a job or career. Relevant caveat: a calling orientation is partly about how you relate to the work, and people can craft meaning in roles that don't look maximally interesting from the outside.

People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events, including career changes. Both you and your friends are likely overweighting whichever single factor feels salient right now — "interesting work" included.

Person-job and person-organization fit are among the strongest predictors of satisfaction and retention, more so than any single job attribute.
